item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The image you have sourced is under the CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 license and is therefore not compliant with the SCP Wiki's CC-BY-SA 3.0 license. As such, the image has been removed. Make sure to follow this handy guide when picking any future images to make sure the license is compatible.

Make sure to stay away from NC (NonCommercial).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure I get why this is tagged "factory"? While it is a factory, it doesn't seem to have any connection to THE Factory (with a capital F).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, a lot of the prior conceptual SCPs were aided by being very sparse. There wasn't a whole lot going on beyond the core concept anomaly, which left them tight and gave readers mental room to be very disturbing.

With this one, there is a lot going on aside from the core issue, and it isn't very coherently assembled. For examples:
- For the core effect, there's the question of whether the change is perceptual or literal. SCP-1359 drastically alters perceived meaning, but nothing physical. I think Allison Eckhart is also perceptual. SCP-2719 is literal. A lot of your description makes me think it's perceptual, but when you get out on the margins like the continued resupply of the factory and stores, that feels literal/like magic. That's not in-character spray-based conceptual vagueness, that's OOC narratively vague. I could see such vagueness working as a build-up (aka you thought it was just perceptual but then you see the factory and now It's magically putting bottles on shelves, oh shit), but it's written in the opposite direction.

- The paragraphs about the SCP and the ingredients being conceptually vague/mixed didn't make this any clearer or contribute much to the narrative. The SCP being vague would've been a nice mind-screw if it was called out across the entire document, but it didn't seem to be.

- I don't understand how an amnestic effect is a side effect of the conceptual merging, and the .3 addendum did not make this any clearer. It just seemed to show it also happening previously.

- I don't understand why something the supervisor defines as being designed for crazy molecular alchemy would be sold in spray bottles at business supply stores, rather than some kind of super expensive/specialized compound. This lends to the feeling that the containment procedures and paragraphs about the spray aren't well integrated with the core idea or the factory mishap.

- Why isn't the factory mentioned in the procedures? This is a sign of broader flow issues.

- The first description of the factory is sheer genius. I would've upvoted there were it not for the clutter. The later event log was a bit repetitive of this, but the action was fairly cool and it has some very awesome imagery.

No vote. There's some great content here, but It's out of focus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The descriptions are stupendously opaque and yet in a way self-explanatory. I don't know how else to describe it but there it is. The "abstract administrative nightmare" part got an honest-to-goodness laugh out of me. This is a perfect level of mind-screw. I think it's a bit lengthy, but I don't really know how to suggest trimming it. But overall, good enough to get a vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've gone ahead and removed one test log, two emails and a paragraph in the containment procedures. I'm gonna keep thinking of ways to trim this one down guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree with this. There's a cool concept here, but as it stands its length makes it feel too tedious to be enjoyable.

Novoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How would you suggest I go about shortening it? It feels like there is an appropriate buildup for each segment (and overall), and i don't want to lose any of the 'wow' factor in the testing logs.

I think it being long isn't bad, but I can understand that it is on the longer side. Not nearly as much so as many, and the fact that it is +32 on its first day suggests trimming it might not work out for me like i would want.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is great but it’s too long. The tests went for too long, the exploration went too long, and my eyes glazed over in the emails. I’m at a novote for now, which would become an upvote if all three of the addenda got greatly shortened, or some cut.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It occurs to me that this might a good be article for:

Mobile Task Force Epsilon-7 ("Forget-Me-Nots") is to continuously monitor and disrupt civilian development of mnestics.

ETA: Fixed autocorrect error in link.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, I have no intention of shortening it at this point. There's something for everyone according to the comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
observers not on Class-W Mnestic regimens

I don't know what this means.

It means the people who see it, unless they happen to be taking a regular dose of memory enhancement medicine.

"Class-W" appears to be the standard mnestic drug for long-term memory enhancement, first seen in, I think, We Need to Talk about Fifty-Five. Other mnestics are Class-X (retrograde memory enhancement; seen in Unforgettable, That's What You Are) and Class-Z (total recall, resistance to antimemes, no effective safe dose; seen in Your Last First Day).

This implies that Class-Y would be an acute anterograde enhancer (useful for briefings and making observations!), but it hasn't appeared yet to my knowledge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mnestic drugs counteract antimemetic effects. This is touched upon heavily in SCP-3125 and a few others

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
observers not on Class-W Mnestic regimens

I don't know what this means.

Other than that I really didn't care for the testing logs (After the first ~5 I understood what it did and none of the other logs provided me anything new) and the Incident report was very long for me. The emails were excellent, though I think a few could be cut out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Efficacy of the spray has increased by 25% since 2003.

So, it's new and improved? :)

I liked this, and I upvoted, but I want to say, I feel like you could lose the first and last addenda, for all that "I'm not sure we exist anymore" is a great stinger. The experiment logs go on for quite a while, and the emails don't add a whole lot to the overall presentation. It's the exploration that's strongest, and I think it covers everything quite well, considering how esoteric this is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it, test 3533-3 was fantastic.

Testing confirms SCP-3533 successfully diluted into water. Notably, only 28.4ml of solution was measured after agitation, suggesting some material was lost to an unknown vector.

This is possible with non-anomalous liquid. I considered doing this as a lab for my science class last December. I suggest changing it to Mass (grams) which would be properly impossible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this has been a favorite of mine

Thanks to the 10+ people in chat who looked at this
